title: Exam 2 Developmental Psych Practice Test (Harder, 40 MCQs)

questions.prompt: A 10-month-old repeatedly drops a spoon from the high chair and watches the caregiver pick it up, then repeats the action with a grin. In Piaget’s sensorimotor substages, this is best described as:
An infant shows decreased looking time to the same stimulus after repeated presentations, and then increased looking time to a new stimulus. This method is called:
Which statement best captures the neuroconstructivist view of brain development?
options: Brain development is fixed and unfolds the same way regardless of experience
Experience and biology continuously interact to shape developing neural systems

questions.prompt: A caregiver remains warm but becomes suddenly expressionless and unresponsive during an interaction task. The infant quickly shows distress and attempts to re-engage. This describes:
Which pattern would you MOST expect in the Strange Situation for avoidant attachment?
options: High distress at separation; easily soothed at reunion
Little overt distress; avoids or ignores caregiver at reunion
High distress; seeks contact but resists soothing
Freezing, disoriented, or contradictory behaviors especially at reunion

questions.prompt: A parent says, “Yes, that’s a doggie! The doggie is running!” after a toddler says “dog run.” This is best described as:
Which is the BEST example of declarative pointing?
options: Pointing to a cookie to get it
Pointing to a toy to request help opening it
Pointing at an airplane to share interest with a caregiver
Pointing at a door to leave the room

questions.prompt: In Chess & Thomas’s framework, the temperament profile most associated with irregular routines, intense reactions, and negative mood is:
A key prediction from dynamic systems theory about learning to walk is that walking emerges from:
options: The interaction of multiple changing components (body, balance, motivation, environment)
A skill that directly transfers from crawling with no reorganization

questions.prompt: Which pairing is MOST consistent with the typical developmental sequence of neural maturation?
options: Prefrontal cortex develops before primary sensory cortex
Primary sensory/motor areas develop earlier than prefrontal cortex
Language areas fully myelinate before sensory areas
All areas develop at the same rate (no sequence)

questions.prompt: Synaptic pruning is best described as:
options: The formation of new synapses everywhere equally
The elimination of connections that are rarely used, influenced by experience
The coating of axons with myelin
A process that only occurs prenatally

questions.prompt: Myelination primarily supports:
options: Faster and more efficient neural communication

questions.prompt: Lateralization refers to:
options: Specialization of the two cerebral hemispheres for different functions
The elimination of synapses in early childhood
The infant’s preference for one caregiver over another
The shift from dyadic to triadic interaction

questions.prompt: Which statement BEST reflects Kagan’s temperament approach?
options: Temperament is mainly the 9 dimensions like rhythmicity and intensity
Temperament is reactivity plus effortful control across multiple dimensions
Temperament centers on inhibited vs uninhibited responses to novelty
Temperament is defined as attachment security

questions.prompt: Rothbart & Bates emphasize which broad temperament dimensions?
The goodness-of-fit model predicts the best outcomes when:
options: Parenting is identical for all children
Parenting style matches the child’s temperament and supports regulation
Children’s temperament is ignored to promote resilience
Caregivers avoid structure to maximize exploration

questions.prompt: Temperament-sensitive parenting for a slow-to-warm-up child would MOST likely include:
options: Sudden exposure to large groups to 'toughen up'
Gradual introductions to new situations with patient support

questions.prompt: Which best indicates a developing sense of self in the second year (especially the second half)?
options: Depth perception on a visual cliff

questions.prompt: The Strange Situation is primarily designed to assess:
options: Attachment quality via responses to separation and reunion

questions.prompt: Which caregiving pattern is MOST associated with avoidant attachment?
Which caregiving pattern is MOST associated with resistant (ambivalent) attachment?
Disorganized attachment is MOST strongly associated with caregiving that is:
Bowlby’s internal working model refers to:
options: An infant’s reflex patterns for feeding
Mental representations of relationships that guide expectations and behavior
A set of speech sounds in phonology

questions.prompt: Which sequence best matches Bowlby’s four phases of attachment development?
A hallmark of clear-cut attachment (around later infancy) is:
options: Separation anxiety and active seeking of the caregiver
Full understanding of caregiver goals and plans

questions.prompt: Which is the BEST example of triadic interaction?
options: Infant and caregiver coordinate attention toward a toy while sharing affect

questions.prompt: Harlow’s studies with rhesus monkeys most strongly supported the idea that attachment is driven by:
Intermodal perception is best defined as:
options: Perceiving depth using binocular cues only
Integrating information across senses (e.g., matching what is seen with what is heard/felt)

questions.prompt: A long-term concern with early untreated cataracts is that they can:
options: Have no lasting effects if fixed later
Disrupt early visual input and lead to lasting perceptual deficits
Improve depth perception by increasing reliance on touch

questions.prompt: Perceptual narrowing in the first year is MOST likely to show up as:
options: Increasing ability to discriminate all phonemes/faces equally
Greater specialization for familiar phonemes/faces and reduced discrimination of unfamiliar ones

questions.prompt: Which pairing best matches a language subsystem with what it studies?
A morpheme is:
options: A gesture used in joint attention

questions.prompt: Telegraphic speech is characterized by:
options: Mostly function words (e.g., the, is, at)
Short utterances with key content words, missing many grammatical markers

questions.prompt: Child-directed speech typically includes:
options: High pitch, exaggerated intonation, simpler structures

questions.prompt: The ‘problem of reference’ in word learning refers to the idea that:
options: A new word could refer to many possible things in the environment

questions.prompt: In Piaget’s theory, a scheme is best described as:
options: An organized pattern of action or thought used to interpret experiences
A method for measuring looking time

questions.prompt: A major criticism of Piaget’s account of object permanence is that:
options: It claims infants have innate object knowledge at birth
It may underestimate infants’ understanding because tasks require motor responses
It only applies to language development

questions.prompt: Baillargeon’s drawbridge (violation-of-expectation) findings most strongly suggest that:
options: Infants may show some understanding of object continuity earlier than Piaget suggested
Object permanence only appears after age 3

questions.prompt: Newborn imitation of facial gestures is considered remarkable because:
options: It suggests very early social responsiveness before much learning experience
